title: Pre-Season Testing Verdict: Zwart and McLaren Impress as Ferrari's Questions Remain
caption: Maikel Zwart topped the final day of testing as Mercedes showed genuine promise.
body:
Three days. Thirty-six sessions. And an enormous amount of information to unpick. Pre-season testing at Bahrain is complete, and while the now-familiar caveats about fuel loads and programme differences will ring loud in certain corners of the paddock, there are patterns in this data that deserve attention regardless. Here is what we learned.

Day 1: McLaren and the Midfield Set the Tone

It was Zhao Lei who set the benchmark on Day 1, his 1:33.126 on softs putting him at the top of the timesheet and immediately underscoring McLaren's status as a team with nothing to prove and everything to defend. The McLaren ran smoothly across 96 laps for Lei, while Joshua Nathanial completed 87 laps further down the order on mediums — a deliberate split suggesting the team used their day efficiently across tyre families. A healthy combined lap count and a name at the very top of the timings is as good a Day 1 as McLaren could have wanted.

Below Lei, the field compressed quickly. Sam Fakt (1:33.307), Julian Kondra (1:33.359), Lukus Wright (1:33.370), and Lucas Koopman (1:33.414) all sat within three tenths on softs — close enough to suggest the midfield is genuinely tight. Koopman's presence near the top on his first day of testing with Williams is notable. The Dutchman, making his full F1CC debut, ran 148 laps without incident and looked composed throughout. Eetu Väisänen placed sixth for Ferrari at 1:33.674, completing 112 laps on a day Ferrari used methodically.

Jason Roy ran the day on mediums, placing eighth at 1:33.857 — a time that masks nothing given the compound; on an equivalent soft-tyre run Roy would almost certainly appear higher up. Richard Hicks, completing his first official F1CC test day, ran mediums for Audi and finished eleventh. Robin Keisner was on hards, clearly working a long-run programme, and placed thirteenth. Afonso Cachinho was absent entirely from Day 1, a note Ferrari and their observers logged carefully.

Day 2: Roy Delivers a Statement

If Day 1 belonged to McLaren, Day 2 was Jason Roy's moment. The newly-promoted Red Bull driver went fastest of all with a 1:33.483 on softs — not a dramatic margin over Lei's Day 1 benchmark, but a clear message that the Red Bull is quick and that Roy is comfortable in it from the first moment. Philippe Verhaeren completed his Day 2 in fourth at 1:33.746, giving Red Bull two names in the top four and a combined lap count suggesting a smooth, productive programme.

Afonso Cachinho finally appeared and ran 176 laps — the highest single-day total up to that point in the test — finishing third at 1:33.603 on softs. No indication of the mechanical problems in the car; the lap count alone suggests the Ferrari ran faultlessly. The context for that 1:33.603 will only be fully understood in Melbourne, but the number itself is hardly alarming for a team with Väisänen as their lead reference point. Wesley Vandesteene completed 144 laps in fifth, going comfortably quicker than his Day 1 time.

Three names lower in the order warranted attention for different reasons. Quarter Kevu placed tenth on mediums at 1:34.587 in his Day 2 run for VCARB, while teammate Lukus Wright logged a separate soft-tyre programme. Michiii Maradöner placed eleventh on mediums for Alpine, 1:34.709, and put in 66 laps, the fewest of Day 2. Alpine are not showing their hand.

Day 3: Zwart Gets the Headlines, Audi Posts a Warning

The final day produced the most significant single result of the three: Maikel Zwart went fastest overall with a 1:33.505 on softs. The former Williams driver, in his first test with Mercedes, topped the day by nearly three tenths from Eren Aygen (1:33.790), who was running for Williams. Mercedes still banked a smooth 152-lap day for Zwart, and that run profile supports the view that the car is reliable and the pace is present.

Robin Keisner placed third for Audi with 1:33.992 on softs — a substantially quicker time than his Day 1 hard-tyre effort and a much more credible competitive reference. After two days of programme work, Audi showed their hand on the final morning: the Keisner lap was real. For a team rebranding from Kick Sauber and carrying the weight of a full manufacturer's expectations, that sort of timing on genuinely representative rubber is the best possible way to close out a test.

Ferrari's Day 3 again produced a quietly complex picture. Cachinho ran mediums this time and went fourth at 1:34.107. Väisänen went fifth on softs at 1:34.160. The Ferrari appears to be a car that can lap in the low 1:34s comfortably but has not yet produced the kind of soft-tyre headline number that McLaren and Mercedes have. Whether that is a programme choice or a genuine pace gap is the central question Ferrari's engineers will be debating ahead of Australia. Kevu did appear on Day 3, running mediums and completing 172 laps as part of VCARB's long-run programme.

The Testing Picture Heading to Melbourne

Reading across all three days, a few observations stand out. McLaren, Red Bull, and Mercedes each produced one day-fastest time and all three ran strong lap-count programmes. Red Bull showed a strong Day 2 marker from Roy, with Verhaeren adding numbers. Audi improved meaningfully across the three days, closing Day 3 in the top three on softs — the clearest upward trajectory of any team in the field. Ferrari's pace is present but the raw soft-tyre headline number has not appeared yet; Cachinho's heavy Day 2 running suggests development work rather than performance validation. Williams' Koopman showed nothing to alarm the team on debut. Haas ran solidly without posting times that set pulses racing.

One notable profile in the overall fastest list is Kevu: he ran only medium-tyre laps across Days 2 and 3 and posted no soft-tyre benchmark. And Jack TEF — now exclusively the Williams Team Principal — did not drive. His transition from cockpit to pit wall is complete.

Three weeks until Albert Park. The laptop analysis has already begun.
